query: are bacteriophages found in the human gut

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Antibiotic Resistance and Infection Control
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteriophages are drivers of bacterial evolution in the human microbiome. Prophage can be induced to switch to the lytic replication cycle by host cell stress, including antimicrobial drugs. Therefore, antibiotic treatments, especially those targeting gut microbial flora, can be expected to alter the viral (phage) microbiome as well. Bacteriophages are not as easily inactivated as vegetative bacterial cells but are vulnerable to UV inactivation, autoclaving, and standard hospital disinfection procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A third clinically relevant aspect of bacteriophages is that their detection can be used as a biomarker for the presence of their host in a complex environmental sample. This is most commonly used as a surrogate for fecal contamination of water sources. If the phage is present, the host most likely is as well. Alternatively, phages have been engineered to produce a detectable molecule, such as luciferase, when they infect their host to detect bacteria in a mixed environmental sample. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Although bacteriophages cannot infect and replicate in human cells, they are an important part of the human microbiome and a critical mediator of genetic exchange between pathogenic and nonpathogenic bacteria. [5] [6] The transfer of genes from 1 bacterial strain to another by a bacteriophage is called transduction and can occur in a generalized or specific manner.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Phages are clinically significant for several reasons. First, many highly pathogenic bacterial toxins are encoded by bacteriophage genomes, such that the host bacterium is only pathogenic when lysogenized by the toxin-encoding phage. Examples are the cholera toxin in Vibrio cholerae [10] , the diphtheria toxin in Corynebacterium diphtheriae , [11] botulinum neurotoxin in Clostridium botulinum, [12] the binary toxin of Clostridium difficile, [13] and Shiga toxin of Shigella species. [10] [14] [10] . Without their phage-encoded toxins, these bacterial species are either much less pathogenic or not. Why phages encode these toxins is not known. While cholera toxin arguably helps both the phage and its host reach their next victim by inducing copious, watery diarrhea, the paralysis resulting from botulinum toxin would seem to have the opposite effect.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteriophages, also known as phages, are viruses that infect and replicate only in bacterial cells. They are ubiquitous in the environment and recognized as the earth's most abundant biological agent. They are extremely diverse in size, morphology, and genomic organization. [1] [2] [3] However, all consist of a nucleic acid genome encased in a shell of phage-encoded capsid proteins, which protect the genetic material and mediate its delivery into the next host cell. Electron microscopy has allowed the detailed visualization of hundreds of phage types, some of which appear to have "heads," "legs," and "tails." Despite this appearance, phages are non-motile and depend upon Brownian motion to reach their targets.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Like all viruses, bacteriophages are very species-specific about their hosts and usually only infect a single bacterial species or even specific strains within a species. Once a bacteriophage attaches to a susceptible host, it pursues 1 of 2 replication strategies: lytic or lysogenic.

retrieved_docs.title: Norovirus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Authors have postulated that noroviruses have a way of bypassing enterocytes to enter the human hosts. Proposed mechanisms include entering through M cells, a specialized type of cell in the gut that overlies the Peyer patches and lymphoid follicles in the gut. M cells lack microvilli and do not secrete mucus, making it easier for the norovirus to enter the host and invade immune cells. However, although definitive data are lacking, other studies suggest noroviruses may directly invade enterocytes lining the gut lumen. Furthermore, the role of the host’s preexisting gut microflora in norovirus infection is being investigated. Experts have proposed that norovirus interacts with bacteria in the gut to enhance infection and replication.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, bacteriophages were the first type of virus discovered and were involved in many of the fundamental discoveries of molecular biology. For example, the proof that DNA was the molecule that transmitted genetic information, the basic mechanisms of gene regulation, and the genetic code, to name but a few, were all discovered using bacteriophages.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Second, bacteriophages are vectors for horizontal gene transfer, including antimicrobial resistance genes. [5] They also have been engineered to introduce genes into specific strains for clinical effect, although this use is currently in the testing stage. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Urinary Tract Infections -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents to modify host defenses are being studied experimentally to reduce recurrent UTIs. [90] [91] Fecal microbiota transfer, which transplants metabolites and microorganisms from the fecal material of a healthy donor, appears promising, and bacteriophage therapy, which targets pathogenic bacteria with specific viruses, is also being explored as a possible therapy in the future. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Large Intestine -- Pathophysiology -- Disorders of Large Intestinal Motility [5] [6]
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious colitis can occur from many different viruses, bacteria, or parasites. Infectious colitis most commonly occurs due to ingestion of contaminated food or water, introducing the infectious organism into the colon. The most common causes are Escherichia coli , Campylobacter , Shigella , and Salmonella . These infectious organisms invade the colon, cause inflammation, and affect the normal function, causing abdominal pain and diarrhea. Clostridium difficile is another organism that can cause colitis in association with antibiotic use. C. difficile is part of healthy, normal flora in the colon but can cause problems if it overgrows. Antibiotic use can destroy other susceptible normal flora in the colon, allowing overgrowth and invasion of C. difficile.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Bacterial Toxin Mediated Disease
retrieved_docs.content: It is important for all team members caring for a patient with a phage-borne toxin-mediated disease, such as cholera or shigella, that disinfection procedures be chosen for their ability to inactivate viruses and bacteria. Even though bacteriophages do not infect human cells directly, they can mediate virulence gene transfer from pathogenic to non-pathogenic bacterial strains.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Introduction -- Lytic Replication
retrieved_docs.content: During a lytic replication cycle, a phage attaches to a susceptible host bacterium, introduces its genome into the host cell cytoplasm, and utilizes the host's ribosomes to manufacture its proteins. The host cell resources are rapidly converted to viral genomes and capsid proteins, which assemble into multiple copies of the original phage. As the host cell dies, it is either actively or passively lysed, releasing the new bacteriophage to infect another host cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterococcus Infections -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Enterococcal cytolysin causes liver cell injury, especially in patients with alcoholic hepatitis, correlating with both-disease severity and mortality. Hence one of the therapies suggested for this patient population is cytolytic enterococci-specific bacteriophages. [76]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteriophages -- Function -- Specialized Transduction
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to genetic exchange, bacteriophages can alter microbial populations because they prey on specific species of bacteria while leaving others unharmed. For more than 100 years, research has attempted to use this property to treat pathogenic bacterial infections in people and animals. Although wild phages probably have transient effects on wild bacterial populations, [7] many obstacles exist to the clinical use of lytic bacteriophages as antimicrobial therapy (phage therapy) in humans. Wild bacterial strains are diverse; many resist 1 or multiple phages. Many resistance mechanisms are known, with one famous example being the CRISPR-Cas9 system, which has been engineered as a tool for genetic manipulation in the lab and originated as a bacterial defense mechanism against bacteriophage infection. [8] In addition, phages are much more immunogenic than antimicrobial drugs and are rapidly cleared from the blood by the reticular endothelial system. Their large size relative to antimicrobial drugs likely limits their use to topical applications if effective phage cocktails are found. Some investigators have suggested that phage enzymes, which can penetrate bacterial cell walls, may be a more straightforward strategy. [9] To date, no randomized, controlled, double-blind trials have shown either strategy's efficacy in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1525
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics are classified as members of the streptomycin group, indicating that streptomycin encompasses these gut‑targeted antimicrobial agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal infection is classified as a bacterial intestinal infection (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي), indicating that the broader category of intestinal infections includes bacterial etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Fidaxomicin is an oral macrocyclic antibiotic that is specifically indicated for the treatment of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis), a severe infection of the colon caused primarily by Clostridioides difficile. By targeting C. difficile while sparing much of the normal intestinal microbiota, fidaxomicin helps eradicate the pathogen and reduce recurrence rates compared with some other agents. Its use is especially valuable in patients with moderate to severe disease or in those who have not responded adequately to first‑line therapies, contributing to improved clinical outcomes and more effective management of this potentially life‑threatening colonic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics refer to a group of antimicrobial agents used to treat infections within the gastrointestinal tract, and this group includes both nystatin and fidaxomicin. Nystatin is an antifungal medication that targets fungal organisms in the gut, while fidaxomicin is a narrow‑spectrum antibacterial drug primarily employed against Clostridioides difficile infection. By classifying nystatin and fidaxomicin as members of the intestinal antibiotic category, clinicians recognize their specific roles in managing gastrointestinal microbial disorders and can select the appropriate agent based on the type of pathogen involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1674
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics are a class of antimicrobial agents that act primarily within the gastrointestinal tract, providing targeted treatment for gut‑related infections while minimizing systemic exposure. Both rifaximin and streptomycin are members of this intestinal antibiotic category, illustrating the range of agents that can be used to address conditions such as travel‑associated diarrhea, hepatic encephalopathy, small‑intestinal bacterial overgrowth, and other gastrointestinal infections. Their inclusion in the intestinal antibiotic group underscores their utility in delivering high local drug concentrations with a reduced risk of systemic side effects, which is an important consideration in patient‑centered antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2055
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole derivatives, intestinal antiinfectives and sulfonamides, intestinal antiinfectives are both specific subclasses of intestinal antiinfectives, the broader category of agents used to treat infections of the gastrointestinal tract. Each of these drug groups is classified as an intestinal antiinfective, indicating their role in eradicating or controlling pathogenic microorganisms within the intestines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2738
retrieved_docs.content: Natamycin is classified as an intestinal antibiotic, being a member of the intestinal antibiotics group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1375
retrieved_docs.content: Amfotericin B, nystatin, and streptomycin are all classified as members of the intestinal antibiotics group, indicating that each of these agents is considered an antibiotic used within the gastrointestinal tract. This grouping reflects their shared therapeutic role in targeting infections of the intestinal environment, allowing clinicians to select among them based on the specific pathogen and clinical context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics encompass agents such as vancomycin and fidaxomicin, both of which are employed in the treatment of pseudomembranous colitis (Clostridioides difficile infection). Vancomycin belongs to the glycopeptide class of antibacterials, a group that also includes the related lipoglycopeptide telavancin. Fidaxomicin, another member of the intestinal antibiotic category, is specifically indicated for managing C. difficile–associated pseudomembranous colitis. Together, these glycopeptide‑derived and related antibiotics provide targeted therapeutic options for patients with severe colonic infections, highlighting the importance of classifying antibiotics by their spectrum and mechanism to guide effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2050
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics, intestinal constitute a group of antimicrobial agents that include both nystatin and streptomycin, indicating that these two drugs are considered members of the intestinal antibiotic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: profile—guide clinicians in selecting appropriate antimicrobial therapy for infections of the eye, throat, gynecologic tract, intestine, skin and systemic sites, and in anticipating potential side effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1278
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics, represented by agents such as vankomycin and fidaxomicin, are classified within the broader group of antibiotics that act primarily in the gastrointestinal tract. Both vankomycin and fidaxomicin are recognized as effective therapeutic options for the management of التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب (pseudomembranous colitis), a severe colonic infection often caused by Clostridioides difficile. Their use highlights the importance of targeted intestinal antimicrobial therapy in treating this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1360
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics constitute a class of antimicrobial agents that act within the gastrointestinal tract. Both fidaxomicin and rifaximin are members of this intestinal antibiotic class. Fidaxomicin is specifically used to treat pseudomembranous colitis (التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب), a severe Clostridioides difficile infection of the colon. Recognizing that fidaxomicin and rifaximin belong to the same intestinal antibiotic group assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for gastrointestinal infections and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria are identified as a component of the detailed microbiological assessment labeled “细菌.碳青酶烯类耐药性 已鉴定的:存在与否或特征标识:时间点:肛门:名义型:微生物特异性培养,” which encompasses the detection of carbapenem resistance in bacterial isolates, the presence or absence of specific resistance markers, timing of specimen collection, rectal sampling sites, nominal classification, and culture‑specific methodology. In addition, Bacteria are linked to the entity “botulotoxiny” through a hierarchical “has parent” relationship, indicating that bacteria occupy a parent or upstream position within the classification of this toxin‑related concept. Together, these connections illustrate how bacteria are central both to the evaluation of antimicrobial resistance in clinical specimens and to the taxonomic organization of toxin‑related entities, underscoring their importance in microbiology‑driven patient care and infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal antibiotics refer to a group of antimicrobial agents used within the gastrointestinal tract, and this group includes nystatin, streptomycin, and fidaxomicin. Nystatin, although primarily classified as an antifungal, is employed to treat fungal infections of the intestine. Streptomycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that targets bacterial pathogens in the gut. Fidaxomicin is a macrocyclic antibiotic specifically indicated for the treatment of Clostridioides difficile infection, which presents clinically as pseudomembranous colitis; consequently, fidaxomicin may be used to treat pseudomembranous colitis. Understanding that these diverse agents—nystatin, streptomycin, and fidaxomicin—are all members of the intestinal antibiotic category assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy based on the underlying microbial cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1331
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics, intestinal, are classified as members of the nystatin group, indicating that these intestinal‑targeted antimicrobial agents are grouped with nystatin, a polyene antifungal commonly used to treat gastrointestinal fungal infections.
